title: Java File I/O Streams
content:
### Java I/O Streams

Streams represent a sequence of data. Java uses streams for input/output operations.

---

### Stream Hierarchy

```
InputStream (byte-oriented)
  |-- FileInputStream
  |-- BufferedInputStream
  |-- ObjectInputStream

OutputStream (byte-oriented)
  |-- FileOutputStream
  |-- BufferedOutputStream
  |-- ObjectOutputStream

Reader (character-oriented)
  |-- FileReader
  |-- BufferedReader
  |-- InputStreamReader

Writer (character-oriented)
  |-- FileWriter
  |-- BufferedWriter
  |-- OutputStreamWriter
```

---

### Byte Streams vs Character Streams

| Aspect | Byte Stream | Character Stream |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| Classes | InputStream/OutputStream | Reader/Writer |
| Data | Binary files | Text files |
| Encoding | Raw bytes | Uses charset |

---

### Standard Streams

| Stream | Object |
|--------|--------|
| System.in | InputStream (keyboard) |
| System.out | PrintStream (console output) |
| System.err | PrintStream (error output) |

---

### Buffered Streams

Buffered streams improve performance by reducing disk I/O:

```java
BufferedInputStream bis = new BufferedInputStream(new FileInputStream("file.dat"));
BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("file.txt"));
```

> **Key Point:** Always close streams to release resources. Use try-with-resources for automatic closing. Byte streams for binary data, character streams for text data.

quiz.question: Which classes should be used for text files?
What is the benefit of buffered streams?
